title: A Hidden Forest Under the Sea
summary: Long ago, there was a land called Doggerland under the North Sea. Scientists found that forests grew there over 16,000 years ago, which is much earlier than they thought. These forests had trees like oak, elm, and hazel. Doggerland was a land bridge connecting Britain to Europe before it sank. The forests could have been a home for animals and maybe early humans. Researchers studied old DNA from mud samples to learn about this lost world. They discovered that some trees survived longer than expected. This helps us understand how plants and animals lived during the Ice Age. Doggerland might have been a safe place for life before it flooded.

keywords.explanation: a land that used to be above water but is now under the North Sea
areas with many trees growing together
tiny parts in mud that tell us about old plants and animals
a very cold time long ago when ice covered much of the Earth
a strip of land that connects two bigger lands, like a bridge
covered by water, like when the sea rises

options: It could have been a home with forests
questions.correct_answer: It could have been a home with forests

background_read: Doggerland was a real place that existed thousands of years ago, connecting what is now Britain to Europe. It was above water during the last Ice Age, when much of the world was colder. Scientists study it to understand how plants, animals, and humans lived long ago. Forests are important because they provide food and shelter. The North Sea formed when sea levels rose and covered Doggerland. Learning about lost lands helps us see how Earth changes over time.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from the University of Warwick
WHAT: Found forests in Doggerland over 16,000 years ago
WHERE: Under the North Sea, between Britain and Europe
WHY: To learn about ancient environments and how they supported life

perspectives.description: Scientists use new methods to uncover old secrets about Earth's history.
